title: Raspberry leaf tea
subtitle: A uterine tonic for the third trimester
excerpt: A simple infusion of dried raspberry leaf. Traditionally introduced in the third trimester of pregnancy (from approximately 32 weeks) to support uterine tone in preparation for labour. Stage-specific: not for the first or second trimester.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, raspberry entry: the uterine tonic tradition, the leaf preparation. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Rubus idaeus, the Eclectic use as a uterine tonic and parturient. the European herbal standard position on Rubi idaei folium for traditional herbal medicinal products.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried raspberry leaf stores 12 months in a sealed tin. Prepare each cup fresh.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb traditionally used to tone and strengthen the uterine muscle. In western herbal practice, a uterine tonic is introduced only in late pregnancy, never in the first or second trimester when uterine stimulation could cause harm.

An alkaloid in raspberry leaf traditionally associated with its uterine-toning action. It is classified as an antispasmodic on smooth muscle in some studies, which is the proposed mechanism for toning uterine contractions.

content.text: Raspberry leaf (Rubus idaeus) is the most commonly used
in the western herbal tradition. Its

and tannin content are the active compounds traditionally associated with its toning effect on the uterine muscle. The preparation is a simple hot infusion. The key point: raspberry leaf is introduced only in the third trimester (from around 32 weeks). It is never used in the first or second trimester. This is not a herb with general safe-in-pregnancy status; it is a herb taken at a specific time, for a specific purpose, after discussion with a midwife.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Stage-specific use only. Do not use raspberry leaf in the first or second trimester. Traditional western herbal practice introduces raspberry leaf from approximately 32 weeks of pregnancy as preparation for labour. Using it earlier carries the risk of stimulating premature uterine activity.

Discuss with your midwife before starting. This is a herb with a specific clinical use case that should be agreed with your midwife, who knows your individual pregnancy history. Readers with a history of preterm labour, fast labour, multiple pregnancy, or any pregnancy complication should discuss before use.

Not for cycle support in non-pregnant readers. Raspberry leaf's traditional uses include general cycle support as a uterine tonic outside pregnancy. This preparation is specifically for third-trimester use; see a herbalist for the non-pregnancy cycle-support dosing.

items.prepNote: dried raspberry leaf, about 1 teaspoon; from a reputable herbal supplier

content.text: Place the dried raspberry leaf in a covered teapot. Pour over the just-boiled water. Put the lid on and steep for 10 minutes. The infusion should be pale amber-red and slightly astringent to taste. Strain into a cup. Drink warm.

One cup per day from 32 weeks, increasing to two cups per day from 36 weeks if your midwife is in agreement. This is the traditional western herbal gradual-introduction approach. the herbal pharmacopoeia dose is 4-8 g dried leaf daily. Start at the lower end; increase if no adverse effects are noticed. Stop at the onset of labour. Introduce the starting dose with your midwife's knowledge.
Prepare each cup fresh. Dried raspberry leaf stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark.
When not to use this remedy

Do not use in the first or second trimester. Do not use in complicated pregnancy or breech presentation without midwife guidance. Stop if you notice unusual uterine activity after starting raspberry leaf and contact your midwife.
